worth_it_explanation: Floor pricing sets the price below which a video or Connected TV (CTV) impression goes unsold, so it controls the trade between fill rate and the value of each ad. A floor that leans on one flat number across every content type, format, and device, or one that accepts a dynamic floor without a fixed baseline to measure against, can leave revenue on the table without anyone noticing. The check is a review of the floor rules a publisher already holds in FreeWheel, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete pricing decision the publisher can test.
intro: Floor pricing decides the minimum price FreeWheel accepts for a video or CTV impression, which sets where the line falls between filling more inventory and protecting the value of each ad. FreeWheel serves premium video, so the inventory spans linear-style ad pods, on-demand pre-roll and mid-roll, and CTV apps, and the price each commands differs along several lines. These checks confirm that the floor strategy is a deliberate, evidence-based choice rather than a default applied across the board, and that dynamic floors are measured against a fixed baseline rather than accepted without comparison.

audit_checks.title: Floors are not segmented by inventory, format, geography, or device
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A single flat floor across all video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory ignores that some content, formats, countries, and devices clear at higher prices than others. A flat floor either underprices the premium inventory, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the thin inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot serve inventory whose value varies along every one of those lines.

educate.base:
A floor price works best when it matches what the impression is worth, and impressions are not worth the same across a publisher's video inventory. A mid-roll slot inside premium long-form content clears higher than a pre-roll on a short clip. A live sports ad pod carries different demand than a library on-demand title. Traffic from a high-spending country clears higher than traffic from a market where advertiser demand is thin. CTV app inventory, mobile web video, and desktop video each carry their own typical clearing price. A floor set as one flat number across all of this cannot fit inventory whose value varies that widely.

The cost of a flat floor cuts both ways. Set it to the average and it sits below the premium inventory, so impressions that could have cleared higher clear at the floor and the difference is left uncollected. Set it higher to protect the premium inventory and it sits above the thin inventory, so impressions that would have sold at a lower price go unsold instead. Either way, one number serves the middle and misprices the ends.

FreeWheel lets a publisher set floor prices that target the dimensions that move clearing price, so floors can differ by inventory source, ad placement within the pod, geography, and device rather than one floor for everything. In programmatic demand, the floor a publisher sets flows to bidders as the OpenRTB 'bidfloor' on the bid request, and for variable-length video FreeWheel can express pricing that scales with ad duration rather than a single CPM. The audit question is whether the floors in place reflect those value differences or whether a single flat floor is doing work that several segmented floors should do.

A few signs point to floors that are not segmented:
  • One floor value covers all inventory, with no breakout by content, format, geography, or device
  • Reporting shows average clearing prices that differ widely across content, placements, countries, or devices while the floor stays the same for all of them
  • Premium long-form or live inventory clears at or near the same floor as filler inventory, which suggests the floor is not protecting its value
You may want to confirm that the floors reflect the price differences the inventory actually shows. One approach is to read average clearing price by inventory source, format, geography, and device, then set floors that follow those differences rather than a single number across the board.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, live sports and premium long-form pods usually clear higher than short on-demand clips, and audience from high-demand markets clears higher than audience from thin ones. You may want to set floors by content type, placement, and geography so the premium video holds its value while the lower-value inventory keeps a floor low enough to stay filled.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content property, product demos and shoppable video reach an audience closer to a purchase and can clear higher than general editorial video, and device matters because mobile and CTV viewers carry different demand. You may want to segment floors so the commercial, higher-intent video carries a higher floor than the editorial video rather than sharing one number.

learn_more.source_title: Better Floors for Variable Duration Video and Audio Ads

investigate.intro: Review the floor values across inventory sources, formats, geographies, and device categories to see whether they are differentiated or whether one flat floor covers everything.

freewheel_ui.label: FreeWheel console
steps: Open the deals and pricing area of the FreeWheel console and list the active floor rules with their values.
For each rule, note which inventory sources, ad placements within the pod, geographies, and device categories it covers and at what floor.
Record whether the floors differ across content, format, geography, and device, or whether a single floor applies across all inventory with no breakout.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by inventory source, then by content or format, then by country, then by device category, with average CPM and fill rate.
Compare the average clearing price across those dimensions to see how much it varies between premium and thin inventory.
Note any case where clearing prices differ widely but the floor stays the same, which points to inventory that a segmented floor could price more accurately.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the floor values across inventory sources, formats, geographies, and device categories alongside the average clearing price for each. Floors that follow the value differences the reporting shows, with higher floors on inventory that clears higher and lower floors on inventory that clears lower, pass. A single flat floor applied across inventory whose clearing prices differ widely is a fail recorded with the dimensions where the floor and the value diverge.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor values and the average clearing price by inventory source, format, geography, and device above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} applies a single flat floor across video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory whose value differs by content, format, geography, and device. A flat floor either underprices the premium inventory, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the thin inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot fit inventory that clears at different prices. {{voice_recommend}} reading the average clearing price by inventory source, format, geography, and device in FreeWheel, then setting floor rules that carry higher floors on the inventory that clears higher and lower floors on the inventory that clears lower. After the change, recheck fill rate and average price across the segments to confirm the floors fit the value of each.

audit_checks.title: Dynamic floors are accepted without evaluating them against a fixed baseline
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A dynamic floor that adjusts per request often raises revenue, but it optimizes toward yield, which does not always match a publisher's strategy, for example protecting a premium price point on long-form or live inventory. Accepting it without comparing it to a fixed-floor baseline leaves the choice untested, so a publisher cannot tell whether the dynamic floor is helping or trading away inventory value on a slice of inventory.

educate.base:
FreeWheel gives a publisher more than one way to set a floor. A fixed floor is a number the publisher chooses and FreeWheel applies as written, so the floor holds steady regardless of the demand on any single request. A dynamic floor instead adjusts per request, raising or lowering the minimum the publisher will accept based on signals about likely demand, with the goal of clearing the impression at the highest price it can support. Dynamic floors often raise revenue because they capture value on requests where demand is strong and relax the floor on requests where holding it firm would only lose the sale.

The risk is not that a dynamic floor is wrong. The risk is accepting it without a comparison. A dynamic floor optimizes toward yield, and that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy. A broadcaster protecting a premium rate on live sports, or holding a deliberate floor to keep a direct-sold price intact, may find a dynamic floor clears impressions below the price they meant to protect. Variable ad duration adds another layer because a fifteen-second spot and a thirty-second spot do not carry the same value, and a floor that does not account for duration can misprice one to serve the other. Without a baseline to measure against, the publisher has no way to see any of that happening.

A controlled comparison turns the floor choice into a measured result. A publisher can apply a dynamic floor to a slice of inventory, hold a fixed floor on a comparable slice, and read revenue, fill rate, and average clearing price across both over a window long enough to be stable. That replaces a default that was never questioned with evidence the publisher can act on.

A few signs point to a dynamic floor being accepted rather than evaluated:
  • Dynamic floors run across all inventory with no fixed-floor comparison on record
  • No experiment or before-and-after read exists showing how the dynamic floor performed against a fixed baseline
  • Variable-duration video runs on one floor that does not scale with ad length, so the floor cannot reflect the value difference between short and long spots
You may want to confirm that the dynamic-versus-fixed choice was made on evidence rather than by default. One approach is to hold a fixed-floor baseline on part of the inventory long enough to compare it against the dynamic floor on the rest.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster property, premium placements such as a live sports ad pod often carry a price the publisher set on purpose to protect direct-sold value. You may want to keep a fixed-floor baseline on those slots while testing a dynamic floor elsewhere, so the comparison shows whether the dynamic floor respects the premium or clears below it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content property, shoppable and product video can command higher prices than general editorial video because the audience is closer to a purchase. You may want to compare a dynamic floor against a fixed floor on the commercial video specifically, rather than judging it on a blended average that the lower-value editorial inventory pulls down.

learn_more.source_title: Better Floors for Variable Duration Video and Audio Ads

investigate.intro: Review which floor rules use a dynamic floor versus a fixed floor, look for any test result that compares them, and check whether variable-duration video carries a floor that scales with ad length.

freewheel_ui.label: FreeWheel console
steps: Open the deals and pricing area of the FreeWheel console and list the active floor rules.
For each rule, note whether its floor is fixed or dynamic, and whether the pricing scales with ad duration for variable-length video.
Record which slices of inventory run on a dynamic floor and which, if any, run on a fixed floor that could serve as a comparison baseline.
Ask the publisher whether any experiment or before-and-after comparison was run, and record what the result showed and when it was measured.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the floor rule or the inventory it targets, with revenue, impressions, fill rate, and average CPM.
Compare a slice running a dynamic floor against a comparable slice on a fixed floor, if one exists, to see whether the dynamic floor raised revenue or cleared at a lower price.
Note whether the data supports the current floor choice or whether no fixed-floor slice exists to compare against.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per floor rule, whether the floor is fixed or dynamic, whether variable-duration video carries a floor that scales with ad length, and whether any comparison between a dynamic floor and a fixed baseline exists with a result on record. A setup where the dynamic-versus-fixed choice is deliberate and backed by a comparison passes. A setup where dynamic floors are applied across inventory with no fixed-floor comparison and no stated reason for the choice is a fail recorded with the rules and inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor type for each rule and whether a dynamic-versus-fixed comparison exists above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} applies dynamic floor pricing without a fixed-floor comparison on record, so the choice between a dynamic and a fixed floor has not been tested. A dynamic floor in FreeWheel adjusts per request to maximize yield, which often helps, but that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy, such as protecting a premium rate on live or long-form inventory, and without a baseline the difference goes unmeasured. {{voice_recommend}} holding a fixed-floor baseline on a slice of comparable inventory, running the dynamic floor on the rest, and comparing revenue, fill rate, and average price across both before settling on a floor strategy. After the comparison, keep whichever floor the data supports for each slice of inventory.
